speaker: ARMADO
text: Warble, child, make passionate my sense of

speaker: ARMADO
text: hearing.

speaker: BOY
text: Concolinel.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Sweet air. Go, tenderness of years.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Take this key, give enlargement to the

speaker: ARMADO
text: swain, bring him festinately hither. I must employ

speaker: ARMADO
text: him in a letter to my love.

speaker: BOY
text: Master, will you win your love with a French

speaker: BOY
text: brawl?

speaker: ARMADO
text: How meanest thou? Brawling in French?

speaker: BOY
text: No, my complete master, but to jig off a tune at the

speaker: BOY
text: tongue’s end, canary to it with your feet, humor it

speaker: BOY
text: with turning up your eyelids, sigh a note and sing a

speaker: BOY
text: note, sometimes through the throat as if you

speaker: BOY
text: swallowed love with singing love, sometimes

speaker: BOY
text: through the nose as if you snuffed up love by

speaker: BOY
text: smelling love; with your hat penthouse-like o’er the

speaker: BOY
text: shop of your eyes, with your arms crossed on your

speaker: BOY
text: thin-belly doublet like a rabbit on a spit; or your

speaker: BOY
text: hands in your pocket like a man after the old

speaker: BOY
text: painting; and keep not too long in one tune, but a

speaker: BOY
text: snip and away. These are compliments, these are

speaker: BOY
text: humors; these betray nice wenches that would be

speaker: BOY
text: betrayed without these, and make them men of

speaker: BOY
text: note—do you note me?—that most are affected

speaker: BOY
text: to these.

speaker: ARMADO
text: How hast thou purchased this experience?

speaker: BOY
text: By my penny of observation.

speaker: ARMADO
text: But O— but O—.

speaker: BOY
text: “The hobby-horse is forgot.”

speaker: ARMADO
text: Call’st thou my love “hobby-horse”?

speaker: BOY
text: No, master. The hobby-horse is but a colt,

speaker: BOY
text: and your love perhaps a hackney.—But have you

speaker: BOY
text: forgot your love?

speaker: ARMADO
text: Almost I had.

speaker: BOY
text: Negligent student, learn her by heart.

speaker: ARMADO
text: By heart and in heart, boy.

speaker: BOY
text: And out of heart, master. All those three I will

speaker: BOY
text: prove.

speaker: ARMADO
text: What wilt thou prove?

speaker: BOY
text: A man, if I live; and this “by, in, and without,”

speaker: BOY
text: upon the instant: “by” heart you love her, because

speaker: BOY
text: your heart cannot come by her; “in” heart you love

speaker: BOY
text: her, because your heart is in love with her; and

speaker: BOY
text: “out” of heart you love her, being out of heart that

speaker: BOY
text: you cannot enjoy her.

speaker: ARMADO
text: I am all these three.

speaker: BOY
text:
And three times as much more,
                 
                aside
               and yet

speaker: BOY
text: nothing at all.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Fetch hither the swain. He must carry me a

speaker: ARMADO
text: letter.

speaker: BOY
text: A message well sympathized—a horse to be ambassador

speaker: BOY
text: for an ass.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Ha? Ha? What sayest thou?

speaker: BOY
text: Marry, sir, you must send the ass upon the horse,

speaker: BOY
text: for he is very slow-gaited. But I go.

speaker: ARMADO
text: The way is but short. Away!

speaker: BOY
text: As swift as lead, sir.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Thy meaning, pretty ingenious?

speaker: ARMADO
text: Is not lead a metal heavy, dull, and slow?

speaker: BOY
text: Minime, honest master, or rather, master, no.

speaker: ARMADO
text: I say lead is slow.

speaker: BOY
text: You are too swift, sir, to say so.

speaker: BOY
text: Is that lead slow which is fired from a gun?

speaker: ARMADO
text: Sweet smoke of rhetoric!

speaker: ARMADO
text: He reputes me a cannon, and the bullet, that’s he.—

speaker: ARMADO
text: I shoot thee at the swain.

speaker: BOY
text: Thump, then, and I flee.

speaker: ARMADO
text: A most acute juvenal, voluble and free of grace.

speaker: ARMADO
text: By thy favor, sweet welkin, I must sigh in thy face.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Most rude melancholy, valor gives thee place.

speaker: ARMADO
text: My herald is returned.

speaker: BOY
text: A wonder, master!

speaker: BOY
text: Here’s a costard broken in a shin.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Some enigma, some riddle. Come, thy l’envoi begin.

speaker: COSTARD
text: No egma, no riddle, no l’envoi, no salve in

speaker: COSTARD
text: the mail, sir. O, sir, plantain, a plain plantain! No

speaker: COSTARD
text: l’envoi, no l’envoi, no salve, sir, but a plantain.

speaker: ARMADO
text: By virtue, thou enforcest laughter; thy silly

speaker: ARMADO
text: thought, my spleen. The heaving of my lungs

speaker: ARMADO
text: provokes me to ridiculous smiling. O pardon me,

speaker: ARMADO
text: my stars! Doth the inconsiderate take salve for

speaker: ARMADO
text: l’envoi, and the word l’envoi for a salve?

speaker: BOY
text: Do the wise think them other? Is not l’envoi a salve?

speaker: ARMADO
text: No, page, it is an epilogue or discourse to make plain

speaker: ARMADO
text: Some obscure precedence that hath tofore been sain.

speaker: ARMADO
text: I will example it:

speaker: ARMADO
text: The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee

speaker: ARMADO
text: Were still at odds, being but three.

speaker: ARMADO
text: There’s the moral. Now the l’envoi.

speaker: BOY
text: I will add the l’envoi. Say the moral again.

speaker: ARMADO
text: The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee

speaker: ARMADO
text: Were still at odds, being but three.

speaker: BOY
text: Until the goose came out of door

speaker: BOY
text: And stayed the odds by adding four.

speaker: BOY
text: Now will I begin your moral, and do you follow with

speaker: BOY
text: my l’envoi.

speaker: BOY
text: The fox, the ape, and the humble-bee

speaker: BOY
text: Were still at odds, being but three.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Until the goose came out of door,

speaker: ARMADO
text: Staying the odds by adding four.

speaker: BOY
text: A good l’envoi, ending in the goose. Would you

speaker: BOY
text: desire more?

speaker: COSTARD
text: The boy hath sold him a bargain—a goose, that’s flat.—

speaker: COSTARD
text: Sir, your pennyworth is good, an your goose be fat.

speaker: COSTARD
text: To sell a bargain well is as cunning as fast and loose.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Let me see: a fat l’envoi—ay, that’s a fat goose.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Come hither, come hither. How did this argument begin?

speaker: BOY
text: By saying that a costard was broken in a shin.

speaker: BOY
text: Then called you for the l’envoi.

speaker: COSTARD
text: True, and I for a plantain. Thus came your

speaker: COSTARD
text: argument in. Then the boy’s fat l’envoi, the goose

speaker: COSTARD
text: that you bought; and he ended the market.

speaker: ARMADO
text: But tell me, how was there a costard broken

speaker: ARMADO
text: in a shin?

speaker: BOY
text: I will tell you sensibly.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Thou hast no feeling of it, Mote. I will speak

speaker: COSTARD
text: that l’envoi.

speaker: COSTARD
text: I, Costard, running out, that was safely within,

speaker: COSTARD
text: Fell over the threshold and broke my shin.

speaker: ARMADO
text: We will talk no more of this matter.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Till there be more matter in the shin.

speaker: ARMADO
text: Sirrah Costard, I will enfranchise thee.

speaker: COSTARD
text: O, marry me to one Frances! I smell some

speaker: COSTARD
text: l’envoi, some goose, in this.

speaker: ARMADO
text: By my sweet soul, I mean, setting thee at

speaker: ARMADO
text: liberty, enfreedoming thy person. Thou wert immured,

speaker: ARMADO
text: restrained, captivated, bound.

speaker: COSTARD
text: True, true; and now you will be my purgation,

speaker: COSTARD
text: and let me loose.

speaker: ARMADO
text: I give thee thy liberty, set thee from durance,

speaker: ARMADO
text: and, in lieu thereof, impose on thee nothing but

speaker: ARMADO
text: this: bear this significant to the country maid

speaker: ARMADO
text:
Jaquenetta.
                 
                (
                He
                 
                gives
                 
                him
                 
                a
                 
                paper
                .
                )
               There is remuneration

speaker: ARMADO
text:
(
                giving
                 
                him
                 
                a
                 
                coin
                ,
                )
               for the best ward of

speaker: ARMADO
text: mine honor is rewarding my dependents.—Mote,

speaker: ARMADO
text: follow.

speaker: BOY
text: Like the sequel, I. Signior Costard, adieu.

speaker: COSTARD
text: My sweet ounce of man’s flesh, my incony Jew!

speaker: COSTARD
text: Now will I look to his remuneration.

speaker: COSTARD
text: “Remuneration”! O, that’s the Latin word for

speaker: COSTARD
text: three farthings. Three farthings—remuneration.

speaker: COSTARD
text: “What’s the price of this inkle?” “One penny.” “No,

speaker: COSTARD
text: I’ll give you a remuneration.” Why, it carries it!

speaker: COSTARD
text: Remuneration. Why, it is a fairer name than “French

speaker: COSTARD
text: crown.” I will never buy and sell out of this word.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: My good knave Costard, exceedingly well

speaker: BEROWNE
text: met.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Pray you, sir, how much carnation ribbon

speaker: COSTARD
text: may a man buy for a remuneration?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: What is a remuneration?

speaker: COSTARD
text: Marry, sir, halfpenny farthing.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Why then, three farthing worth of silk.

speaker: COSTARD
text: I thank your Worship. God be wi’ you.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Stay, slave, I must employ thee.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: As thou wilt win my favor, good my knave,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Do one thing for me that I shall entreat.

speaker: COSTARD
text: When would you have it done, sir?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This afternoon.

speaker: COSTARD
text: Well, I will do it, sir. Fare you well.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Thou knowest not what it is.

speaker: COSTARD
text: I shall know, sir, when I have done it.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Why, villain, thou must know first.

speaker: COSTARD
text: I will come to your Worship tomorrow

speaker: COSTARD
text: morning.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: It must be done this afternoon. Hark, slave,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: it is but this:

speaker: BEROWNE
text: The Princess comes to hunt here in the park,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And in her train there is a gentle lady.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: When tongues speak sweetly, then they name her name,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And Rosaline they call her. Ask for her,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And to her white hand see thou do commend

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This sealed-up counsel. There’s thy guerdon.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Go.

speaker: COSTARD
text:
Gardon.
                 
                He
                 
                looks
                 
                at
                 
                the
                 
                money
                .
               O sweet

speaker: COSTARD
text: gardon! Better than remuneration, a ’levenpence

speaker: COSTARD
text: farthing better! Most sweet gardon. I will do it, sir,

speaker: COSTARD
text: in print. Gardon! Remuneration!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And I forsooth in love! I that have been love’s whip,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A very beadle to a humorous sigh,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A critic, nay, a nightwatch constable,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A domineering pedant o’er the boy,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Than whom no mortal so magnificent.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This wimpled, whining, purblind, wayward boy,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: This Signior Junior, giant dwarf, Dan Cupid,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Regent of love rhymes, lord of folded arms,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Th’ anointed sovereign of sighs and groans,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Liege of all loiterers and malcontents,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Dread prince of plackets, king of codpieces,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Sole imperator and great general

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Of trotting paritors—O my little heart!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And I to be a corporal of his field

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And wear his colors like a tumbler’s hoop!

speaker: BEROWNE
text: What? I love, I sue, I seek a wife?

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A woman, that is like a German clock,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Still a-repairing, ever out of frame,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And never going aright, being a watch,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: But being watched that it may still go right.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Nay, to be perjured, which is worst of all.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And, among three, to love the worst of all,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: A whitely wanton with a velvet brow,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: With two pitch-balls stuck in her face for eyes.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Ay, and by heaven, one that will do the deed

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Though Argus were her eunuch and her guard.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: And I to sigh for her, to watch for her,

speaker: BEROWNE
text: To pray for her! Go to. It is a plague

speaker: BEROWNE
text: That Cupid will impose for my neglect

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Of his almighty dreadful little might.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Well, I will love, write, sigh, pray, sue, groan.

speaker: BEROWNE
text: Some men must love my lady, and some Joan.
